Course Title: AI and Student Data Privacy Risks
Category: Education & Public Sector: Education, Government Services, Non-Profit organizations
Description: Handling minors’ data in education platforms safely.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how schools or education platforms collect and store student information is helpful, though no technical or legal expertise is required to get started.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key data privacy risks associated with AI tools used in educational settings and explain the legal responsibilities schools have when handling personal data belonging to minors. You will also apply practical strategies to evaluate and organise data protection measures, ensuring that AI-powered education platforms are used safely and in compliance with relevant regulations such as GDPR and COPPA.

lessons.lesson title: AI and Student Data Privacy Risks
lessons.Description: This lesson explores the unique privacy risks that arise when artificial intelligence is used in educational platforms that handle data belonging to children and young people. You will learn how AI systems collect, process, and store student information, and why this creates specific safeguarding responsibilities for educators and school staff. We will look at the key legislation and principles that govern how minors' data must be handled, including practical steps you can take to reduce risk in your setting. By the end of this lesson, you will feel more confident identifying potential data privacy concerns and knowing when to raise them with the appropriate people in your organisation.
lessons.content: Handling minors’ data in education platforms safely.

slides.subtitle: Keeping student data safe online
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools in schools collect and process large amounts of student data every day
• This includes names, ages, learning behaviours, and sometimes location data
• Children's data carries extra legal protections
• Mishandling this data can cause real harm to young people
• Understanding the risks is the first step to keeping students safe

slides.subtitle: Beyond what students type in
slides.slidecontent:
• Keystroke patterns, response times, and browsing habits logged automatically
• Personal details inferred from behaviour, not just entered directly
• Location data captured via device or network
• Emotional states sometimes estimated from facial recognition or typing rhythm
• Third-party vendors often receive this data without schools realising

slides.subtitle: Collect only what you truly need
slides.slidecontent:
• Ask: is this data necessary for the educational task?
• Skip optional data fields whenever possible
• Use anonymous identifiers instead of names where feasible
• Delete data promptly when finished with it
• Less data held means less risk exposure

slides.subtitle: Default configurations often collect excess data
slides.slidecontent:
• Check default settings on any AI platform before use
• Many tools collect birthdates, locations, and contact details unnecessarily
• Disable features that gather data beyond your learning objectives
• Anonymise or aggregate student responses where possible
• Verify what data gets shared with third parties

slides.subtitle: Who can see what, and why
slides.slidecontent:
• Limit staff access to student data on a need-to-know basis
• Review data holdings regularly to identify outdated information
• Establish clear procedures for data retention and deletion
• Document who has access to which systems and data types
• Regular audits help maintain good data hygiene

slides.subtitle: Daily habits that reduce risk
slides.slidecontent:
• Anonymise data before uploading to any AI platform
• Avoid free, unvetted tools without clear privacy policies
• Check platform privacy policies before adoption
• Verify your school's consent procedures are being followed
• Choose institutional-approved tools over ad-hoc solutions

slides.subtitle: When and how to escalate issues
slides.slidecontent:
• Report concerns immediately to your designated safeguarding lead
• Document what you observed factually, without interpretation
• Never attempt to investigate data breaches alone
• Escalate if initial reports receive no response
• Speaking up protects both students and the institution

slides.subtitle: Your next steps in data protection
slides.slidecontent:
• Treat all student data as sensitive by default
• Maintain clear records of data collection purposes and access rights
• Audit one AI feature against your organisation's privacy policy
• Implement regular reviews of data holdings and tool compliance
• Build these practices into routine educational technology use

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on AI and student data privacy risks. As AI tools become more common in schools, they are quietly collecting and processing enormous amounts of information about students, often without teachers or parents fully realising it. We are talking about names, ages, how children learn, how they behave in digital environments, and sometimes even their location. Now, children are not just small adults when it comes to data protection. The law recognises that their information deserves extra care and stronger safeguards. When that data is mishandled, the consequences can be very real, from privacy breaches to potential harm to a young person's wellbeing or future opportunities. So throughout this course, our goal is to help you understand exactly where those risks lie and what you can do about them. Because the more clearly we see the risks, the better placed we are to protect the students in our care.

So let's start by looking at exactly what AI systems are quietly collecting about your students, because it goes much further than most people realise. It is not just the information students type in directly, like their name or their answers. AI tools are also logging things like how fast they type, how long they pause before responding, and which pages they visit. From those patterns alone, the system can start to make inferences about a student's mood, confidence, or even their home situation. Some tools go further still, using facial recognition or typing rhythm to estimate emotional states. And if a student is using a school device or the school network, location data can be captured as well. Here is the part that often surprises educators: much of this data does not stay with the AI tool you signed up for. It frequently gets passed along to third-party companies, often without schools being aware that is happening at all.

Data minimisation sounds technical, but the idea is really quite simple: only collect the information you actually need. Before you ask students or staff to provide any data, pause and ask yourself whether it is genuinely necessary for the task at hand. If a field is optional, consider leaving it blank altogether. And think carefully about names in particular. Quite often, you can use anonymous identifiers or reference numbers instead, which removes a lot of risk straight away. Once you have finished with the data, delete it. Do not let it sit around in a folder or a spreadsheet just in case it might be useful one day. The less data you hold, the less there is to go wrong if something is ever breached or misused. It is a straightforward principle, but it makes a real difference in practice.

Most AI platforms come with default settings that collect far more student information than you might expect or need. Before implementing any new tool, take the time to review those settings carefully. Look for options to disable features that gather personal details like birthdates, home addresses, or family contact information unless these are genuinely required for your educational objectives. Many platforms also offer ways to anonymise responses or group them together so individual students cannot be identified. Pay particular attention to what gets shared with external companies. Some platforms automatically send data to analytics firms or advertising networks, and you want to know about that upfront. A few minutes spent reviewing these configurations can save significant privacy headaches later on.

Good data governance means being intentional about who can access student information and why. Not every staff member needs to see all student data, so implement access controls that match people's actual responsibilities. It is also crucial to review what data you are holding on a regular basis. Information that was useful six months ago might no longer be needed, and holding onto it unnecessarily just increases your risk exposure. Establish clear procedures for when data should be deleted and stick to them. Keep documentation about who has access to which systems and what types of student information they can see. This is not just about compliance, it is about creating a culture where student privacy is actively protected rather than just assumed to be safe.

So now that we understand the risks, let's talk about what you can actually do to protect your students. The good news is that a lot of this comes down to simple habits. Before you upload any student information into an AI tool, strip out the names and anything that could identify a specific child. You really want to get into that routine. It's also worth being cautious about free tools you find online, because if there's no clear privacy policy or institutional approval, you genuinely don't know where that data is going. Speaking of which, always take a moment to check the privacy policy of any platform you're considering using. And make sure you're familiar with your school's consent procedures, because they exist for a reason and they're there to protect everyone involved, including you. Small steps, but they really do make a significant difference.

So, what do you actually do if something doesn't feel right? The answer is simple: act straight away. Don't sit on it, don't wait to see if it resolves itself. As soon as you spot a concern, report it. Your first point of contact should always be your designated safeguarding lead. They are trained for exactly this kind of situation, so please don't try to handle it or look into it yourself. That's really important. What you should do is write down what you observed, as accurately and factually as you can, while it's still fresh in your mind. Stick to what you saw or heard, not your interpretation of it. And if you've raised a concern and you feel like nothing is happening, don't let it drop. Escalate it. Keep going until someone takes action. Protecting students is everyone's responsibility, and speaking up is always the right thing to do.

So let's bring everything together with some core principles to carry forward. First, always treat student data as sensitive, even if it seems trivial or harmless on its own. When working with children, that responsibility becomes even greater. Keep clear records of what data you hold, who has access to it, and why. These aren't just good habits, they're the foundation of responsible practice. And here's something practical to do after this lesson: pick one AI feature in your current platform and run it against your school or organisation's data privacy policy. It's a simple step, but it's a really valuable one. Build regular reviews into your routine, because technology changes quickly and what was compliant yesterday might not be tomorrow. The goal is not perfection, but consistent, thoughtful practice that puts student welfare first.

Who is this for: Teachers, School Administrators, Education Technology Coordinators, Student Services Staff, School Data Protection Officers, Education Platform Managers
Team or Dept this is for: Education, IT, Legal, Compliance, Student Services, Administration
